title: ISO 27001 Supplier IAM and SaaS Integration Change Surveillance
exclusions: Planned maintenance traffic changes; approved load testing; cloud auto-scaling events; new integration onboarding periods; health check and monitoring traffic within designed parameters.
evidence: Network security posture report showing segmentation validation, firewall rule compliance, traffic analysis results, and identified deviations with remediation status.

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.15.1.2 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.15.1.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.7: ISO 27001 Supplier IAM and SaaS Integration Change Surveillance.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.7' running on index=azure sourcetype="mscs:azure:auditlog" (activityDisplayName, initiatedBy, targetResources, result, _time), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a test network flow that violates the defined segmentation policy (e.g., cross-VLAN traffic on a restricted port); verify the monitoring search detects the violation within 15 minutes and generates an alert with source, destination, and violated rule reference.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Generate test network traffic conforming to all defined segmentation and firewall policies; verify no violation alert fires and all flows appear as permitted in the monitoring dashboard.

app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110)
spl:
index=azure sourcetype="mscs:azure:auditlog" earliest=-30d
    (activityDisplayName="*consent*" OR activityDisplayName="*Add app*" OR activityDisplayName="*service principal*")
| eval actor=coalesce(initiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName, initiatedBy.app.displayName, "unknown")
| stats count by activityDisplayName, actor, result
| sort - count
description: Supplier relationships must address information security in agreements and monitor service changes. Cloud IAM audit logs for OAuth app consent and service principal changes surface high-risk supplier integrations that could bypass on-prem controls.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes azure and sourcetypes mscs:azure:auditlog. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Scope to tenant IDs for production directories; (2) enrich with `saas_vendor_risk.csv` keyed on `targetResources{}.displayName`; (3) alert on failed `result` spikes or new high-risk vendors; (4) require CAB reference in ServiceNow optional via lookup; (5) quarterly supplier review slide export. Indexes required: `index=azure`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=mscs:azure:auditlog`.
visualization: Table (activity x actor), Bar chart (consent events by vendor), Single value (distinct actors).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Change data model (Change.All_Changes).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Planned vendor maintenance notifications** — Supplier system changes notified through proper channels within contractual notification periods are expected operations.

2. **SaaS feature releases** — Regular product updates from SaaS suppliers that change IAM integration behaviour within documented release schedules.

3. **Certification renewal documentation lag** — Supplier certifications renewed but awaiting updated documentation upload to the vendor management portal.

4. **Multi-tenant platform changes** — Shared platform updates affecting all tenants simultaneously with advance notification per the service agreement.

5. **Supplier M&A transitions** — Supplier corporate changes (acquisitions, mergers) creating documentation gaps during integration periods with continuity agreements.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `azure` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `A.15.1.2`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO 27001 A.15.1.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.7: ISO 27001 Supplier IAM and SaaS Integration Change Surveillance." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `azure` under `mscs:azure:auditlog`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Scope to tenant IDs for production directories. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** enrich with `saas_vendor_risk.csv` keyed on `targetResources{}.displayName`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert on failed `result` spikes or new high-risk vendors. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** require CAB reference in ServiceNow optional via lookup. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** quarterly supplier review slide export. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `count()` across the last 30 days, grouped by `activityDisplayName, actor, result`. For "ISO 27001 Supplier IAM and SaaS Integration Change Surveillance," the aggregation exposes change activity — change frequency, change authorship, and change surface are the dimensions the regulation cares about, not raw events. The aggregation approach is chosen to produce one result row per analytical unit, which is the typical requirement for compliance dashboarding and report generation.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `activityDisplayName, actor, result` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(activityDisplayName) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Change` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Change` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.7: ISO 27001 Supplier IAM and SaaS Integration Change Surveilla]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.7,reg=ISO_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=azure sourcetype=mscs:azure:auditlog earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `activityDisplayName`, `actor`, `result`. Run:

```spl
index=azure sourcetype=mscs:azure:auditlog earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table activityDisplayName, actor, result
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.7: ISO 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`activity x actor`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Distribution (`consent events by vendor`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`distinct actors`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'ISO 27001 current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Supplier IAM and SaaS Integration Change Surveillance. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
